title: UK Truss Mini-Budget → Gilt Crisis / GBP Flash Crash
summary: Chancellor Kwasi Kwarteng's September 23, 2022 'mini-budget' announced £45B of unfunded tax cuts. Gilt markets sold off violently and GBP/USD collapsed to a record low near $1.035 on September 26, 2022. The BoE was forced into emergency gilt purchases to prevent pension fund collapse.
mechanism: The mini-budget was read as a fiscal credibility failure — unfunded cuts at a time of already-high inflation and rising global rates. Gilt yields spiked as markets priced in default risk / monetization risk. LDI pension funds with leveraged gilt positions faced margin calls, requiring emergency BoE intervention. GBP collapsed as confidence in UK fiscal management evaporated.
causal_chain: September 23, 2022: mini-budget announces £45B in unfunded tax cuts
gilt markets sell off sharply; 30-year gilt yield spikes from ~3.5% toward 5%+
GBP/USD falls to record low ~1.035 on September 26
LDI pension funds face margin calls on leveraged gilt collateral
BoE announces emergency gilt purchase programme September 28
Kwarteng fired October 14; Truss resigns October 20

forecast.note: Bearish GBP; bearish gilts (higher yields); bearish UK equities; bullish BoE emergency intervention probability

outcome.realized_note: GBP/USD hit ~1.035 on September 26, 2022. 30-year gilt yields spiked to ~5% before BoE intervention. Multiple pension funds faced insolvency risk. Kwarteng fired, Truss resigned within weeks.
outcome.chart_note: Validated — GBP/USD and gilt yield charts confirm the crisis; extensively documented
outcome.priced_in_note: Some fiscal risk priced after announcement; severity exceeded initial estimates
outcome.reversal_note: Gilt yields recovered sharply after BoE intervention; GBP recovered to ~1.10+ after Truss resignation

title: Greece Capital Controls and Referendum → Euro Tail Risk
summary: Following the breakdown of bailout negotiations, Greece imposed capital controls on June 28, 2015, closed banks, and called a referendum. Markets repriced Euro break-up risk briefly, sending peripheral spreads wider and EUR lower. Greece ultimately stayed in the euro after accepting terms.
mechanism: The collapse of talks and imposition of capital controls resurrected Euro break-up risk that had been suppressed since the 2012 Draghi speech. Markets briefly repriced the probability of Grexit, widening all peripheral spreads and weakening EUR. When Greece ultimately accepted the third bailout, spreads rapidly recompressed.
causal_chain: Negotiations between Greece and creditors break down June 26, 2015
Capital controls imposed June 28; banks closed; ATM withdrawals capped at €60/day
Referendum called for July 5 — OXI (No) wins 61%
Euro peripheral spreads widen; EUR/USD falls; European bank stocks under pressure
After threats of Grexit, Greek parliament accepts third bailout July 13, 2015

forecast.note: Bearish EUR and periphery during acute phase; tail risk of Grexit kept spreads wide; outcome binary on negotiation result

outcome.realized_note: Greek stock market closed for weeks; reopened down ~23% on July 20. Italian and Spanish spreads widened ~40–80bp briefly. EUR/USD fell from ~1.12 to ~1.08 during acute phase.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — EUR/USD and peripheral spread charts confirm the volatility pattern
outcome.priced_in_note: Negotiations had been ongoing for months; capital controls were the cliff-edge event
outcome.reversal_note: Once bailout accepted, spreads rapidly recompressed; EUR stabilized

title: Turkey Lira Crisis → EM Contagion
summary: Turkey's lira collapsed ~18% on August 10, 2018 after President Erdogan's refusal to raise rates despite 15%+ inflation, combined with US sanctions over a detained pastor. USD/TRY hit record highs, causing contagion to other EM currencies (ZAR, ARS, INR) and temporary stress in European banks with Turkish exposure.
second_order_assets: European banks with Turkey exposure (BBVA, UniCredit)
mechanism: Erdogan's unorthodox belief that high rates cause inflation rather than cure it prevented the TCMB from hiking aggressively. US sanctions doubled tariffs on Turkish steel/aluminum, adding pressure. The confluence of political pressure on central bank independence, current account deficit, and FX-denominated debt created a self-reinforcing lira collapse.
causal_chain: Turkey runs large current account deficit funded by FX borrowing
Erdogan publicly pressures TCMB not to raise rates despite rising inflation
US announces tariffs on Turkish steel/aluminum August 10, 2018
TRY collapses ~18% in one session; USD/TRY hits 7.0+
EM contagion: ZAR, ARS, INR all weaken; European banks with Turkish exposure fall
TCMB finally hikes aggressively in September 2018, providing partial stabilization

forecast.note: Bearish TRY; bearish EM basket; bearish European banks with Turkey exposure; gold may benefit from EM safe-haven demand

outcome.realized_note: TRY fell from ~5.0 to ~7.0 against USD in August 2018. Turkish inflation hit 25% by October 2018. BBVA, UniCredit fell 5–10% on Turkey exposure fears.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — TRY chart confirms the crash; EM contagion charts confirm secondary effects
outcome.priced_in_note: Some TRY weakness was priced; August 10 collapse was sharp relative to prior trend
outcome.reversal_note: TRY stabilized temporarily after TCMB 625bp hike in September 2018; resumed weakening in 2021

title: Euro Sovereign Debt Crisis Peak → Italy 7% Yield Threshold
summary: Italian 10-year bond yields crossed 7% on November 9, 2011 — the level at which Greece, Ireland, and Portugal had required bailouts — triggering fears of a systemic Euro break-up. Berlusconi resigned shortly after; the crisis was eventually stabilized by ECB intervention.
mechanism: Italy's combination of high public debt (~120% GDP), low growth, and political dysfunction created a self-reinforcing dynamic: higher yields → higher refinancing costs → worse fiscal outlook → higher yields. The 7% level was the psychological threshold investors used to gauge bailout necessity; Italy was too large to bail out under existing facilities.
causal_chain: European sovereign debt crisis spreads from Greece to Ireland, Portugal through 2010-2011
Italian political dysfunction (Berlusconi scandals) undermines reform credibility
Italian 10-year yield breaks through 7% on November 9, 2011
Berlusconi resigns November 12, 2011; replaced by technocrat Monti
ECB accelerates SMP (Securities Markets Programme) bond purchases to cap yields
Yields stay elevated until Draghi's July 2012 'whatever it takes' speech

forecast.note: Bearish Italian and Spanish bonds; bearish EUR; bearish European banks; elevated tail risk of Euro break-up

outcome.realized_note: Italian 10-year yields hit 7.5% by end of 2011. EUR/USD fell from ~1.40 to ~1.20 by mid-2012. European bank stocks fell 30–50% through the crisis. Stabilization came only with Draghi's July 2012 intervention.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — Italian BTP yield charts and EUR/USD charts confirm the crisis trajectory
outcome.priced_in_note: Rising yields were already a trend; 7% was the key psychological threshold that triggered forced selling
outcome.reversal_note: Yields began recovering after Draghi 'whatever it takes'; BTP 10yr fell below 4% by end of 2012

sources.title: Italian bond yields and Euro crisis 2011

title: Argentina Peso Crisis → IMF Emergency Loan
summary: Argentina's peso fell sharply from May 2018 as capital flight accelerated, forcing the government to seek an IMF emergency credit line of $50B. The peso lost ~50% of its value against USD in 2018. Argentina's central bank was forced to hike rates to 60%+ to stem outflows.
mechanism: Argentina's large fiscal deficit, high USD-denominated debt, and reliance on foreign capital made it vulnerable to the 2018 global EM selloff triggered by Fed tightening and a rising USD. The government's credibility gap — Macri had tried to gradually reduce subsidies but lacked market confidence — led to self-fulfilling capital flight.
causal_chain: Fed tightening in 2018 strengthens USD, pressures EM currencies
Argentina faces large refinancing need with thinning external buffer
Peso begins depreciating rapidly from April 2018
BCRA hikes rates to 40% in May 2018 to stem outflows — insufficient
Argentina requests IMF standby arrangement May 8, 2018 — $50B facility agreed June
Peso continues depreciating; BCRA hikes to 60% by September 2018

forecast.note: Bearish ARS; bearish Argentine sovereign bonds; bearish Latin American EM basket on contagion risk

outcome.realized_note: ARS fell from ~20 to ~40 per USD through 2018 (~50% depreciation). Argentine sovereign bonds traded at distressed levels. BCRA hiked to 60%. Argentina went on to default in 2020.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — ARS/USD chart confirms the depreciation trajectory
outcome.priced_in_note: Some ARS weakness anticipated; pace and extent of collapse exceeded most forecasts
outcome.reversal_note: No sustained recovery; ARS continued weakening through 2019 before default in 2020
